Identification.LongTitle: An Act to enact the Online Harms Act, to amend the Criminal Code, the Canadian Human Rights Act and An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service and to make consequential and related amendments to other Acts
Identification.ShortTitle: An Act to enact the Online Harms Act, to amend the Criminal Code, the Canadian Human Rights Act and An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service and to make consequential and related amendments to other Acts
Identification.RunningHead: An Act to enact the Online Harms Act, to amend the Criminal Code, the Canadian Human Rights Act and An Act respecting the mandatory reporting ...

Provision.Text: Her Excellency the Governor General recommends to the House of Commons the appropriation of public revenue under the circumstances, in the manner and for the purposes set out in a measure entitled âAn Act to enact the Online Harms Act, to amend the Criminal Code, the Canadian Human Rights Act and An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service and to make consequential and related amendments to other Actsâ.

Part 1 of this enactment enacts the Online Harms Act, whose purpose is to, among other things, promote the online safety of persons in Canada, reduce harms caused to persons in Canada as a result of harmful content online and ensure that the operators of social media services in respect of which that Act applies are transparent and accountable with respect to their duties under that Act.
That Act, among other things,

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: establishes the Digital Safety Commission of Canada, whose mandate is to administer and enforce that Act, ensure that operators of social media services in respect of which that Act applies are transparent and accountable with respect to their duties under that Act and contribute to the development of standards with respect to online safety;

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: creates the position of Digital Safety Ombudsperson of Canada, whose mandate is to provide support to users of social media services in respect of which that Act applies and advocate for the public interest in relation to online safety;

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: establishes the Digital Safety Office of Canada, whose mandate is to support the Digital Safety Commission of Canada and the Digital Safety Ombudsperson of Canada in the fulfillment of their mandates;

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: imposes on the operators of social media services in respect of which that Act applies

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: a duty to act responsibly in respect of the services that they operate, including by implementing measures that are adequate to mitigate the risk that users will be exposed to harmful content on the services and submitting digital safety plans to the Digital Safety Commission of Canada,

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: a duty to protect children in respect of the services that they operate by integrating into the services design features that are provided for by regulations,

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: a duty to make content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor and intimate content communicated without consent inaccessible to persons in Canada in certain circumstances, and

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: a duty to keep all records that are necessary to determine whether they are complying with their duties under that Act;

Provision.Label: (e)
Provision.Text: authorizes the Digital Safety Commission of Canada to accredit certain persons that conduct research or engage in education, advocacy or awareness activities that are related to that Act for the purposes of enabling those persons to have access to inventories of electronic data and to electronic data of the operators of social media services in respect of which that Act applies;

Provision.Label: (f)
Provision.Text: provides that persons in Canada may make a complaint to the Digital Safety Commission of Canada that content on a social media service in respect of which that Act applies is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent and authorizes the Commission to make orders requiring the operators of those services to make that content inaccessible to persons in Canada;

Provision.Label: (g)
Provision.Text: authorizes the Governor in Council to make regulations respecting the payment of charges by the operators of social media services in respect of which that Act applies, for the purpose of recovering costs incurred in relation to that Act.
Part 1 also makes consequential amendments to other Acts.
Part 2 amends the Criminal Code to, among other things,

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: create a hate crime offence of committing an offence under that Act or any other Act of Parliament that is motivated by hatred based on certain factors;

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: create a recognizance to keep the peace relating to hate propaganda and hate crime offences;

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: define âhatredâ for the purposes of the new offence and the hate propaganda offences; and

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: increase the maximum sentences for the hate propaganda offences.
It also makes related amendments to other Acts.

Part 3 amends the Canadian Human Rights Act to provide that it is a discriminatory practice to communicate or cause to be communicated hate speech by means of the Internet or any other means of telecommunication in a context in which the hate speech is likely to foment detestation or vilification of an individual or group of individuals on the basis of a prohibited ground of discrimination. It authorizes the Canadian Human Rights Commission to deal with complaints alleging that discriminatory practice and authorizes the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal to inquire into such complaints and order remedies.
Part 4 amends An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service to, among other things,

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: clarify the types of Internet services covered by that Act;

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: simplify the mandatory notification process set out in section 3 by providing that all notifications be sent to a law enforcement body designated in the regulations;

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: require that transmission data be provided with the mandatory notice in cases where the content is manifestly child pornography;

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: extend the period of preservation of data related to an offence;

Provision.Label: (e)
Provision.Text: extend the limitation period for the prosecution of an offence under that Act; and

Provision.Label: (f)
Provision.Text: add certain regulation-making powers.
Part 5 contains a coordinating amendment.

Heading.Label: PART 4
Heading.TitleText: An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service

Provision.Text: His Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows:

Section.Text: The Online Harms Act is enacted as follows:
This Act may be cited as the Online Harms Act.

Subsection.Text: The following definitions apply in this Act.

Definition.Text: childâmeans a person who is under 18 years of age.â(enfant)
Commissionâmeans the Digital Safety Commission of Canada established by section 10.â(Commission)

content that foments hatredâmeans content that expresses detestation or vilification of an individual or group of individuals on the basis of a prohibited ground of discrimination, within the meaning of the Canadian Human Rights Act, and that, given the context in which it is communicated, is likely to foment detestation or vilification of an individual or group of individuals on the basis of such a prohibited ground.â(contenu fomentant la haine)

content that incites violenceâmeans content that actively encourages a person to commit â or that actively threatens the commission of â an act of physical violence against a person or an act that causes property damage, and that, given the context in which it is communicated, could cause a person to commit an act that could cause

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: serious bodily harm to a person;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a personâs life to be endangered; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: serious interference with or serious disruption of an essential service, facility or system.â(contenu incitant Ã la violence)

Definition.Text: content that incites violent extremism or terrorismâmeans content that actively encourages a person to commit â or that actively threatens the commission of â for a political, religious or ideological purpose, an act of physical violence against a person or an act that causes property damage, with the intention of intimidating or denouncing the public or any section of the public or of compelling a person, government or domestic or international organization to do or to refrain from doing any act, and that, given the context in which it is communicated, could cause a person to commit an act that could cause

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: serious bodily harm to a person;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a personâs life to be endangered; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a serious risk to the health or safety of the public or any section of the public.â(contenu incitant Ã lâextrÃ©misme violent ou au terrorisme)

Definition.Text: content that induces a child to harm themselvesâmeans content that advocates self-harm, disordered eating or dying by suicide or that counsels a person to commit or engage in any of those acts, and that, given the context in which it is communicated, could cause a child to inflict injury on themselves, to have an eating disorder or to die by suicide.â(contenu poussant un enfant Ã se porter prÃ©judice)
content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivorâmeans

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation that shows a child, or a person depicted as being a child, who is engaged in or depicted as being engaged in explicit sexual activity;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation that depicts the sexual organs or anal region of a child, if it is reasonable to suspect that the representation is created or communicated for a sexual purpose;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: written material or an audio recording whose dominant characteristic is the description, presentation or representation of explicit sexual activity with a child, if it is reasonable to suspect that the material or recording is created or communicated for a sexual purpose;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation, written material or an audio recording that shows, describes, presents or represents any of the following, if it is reasonable to suspect that the representation, material or recording is created or communicated for a sexual purpose:

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: a person touching, in a sexual manner, directly or indirectly, with a part of their body or with an object, any part of the body of a child or a person depicted as being a child,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a person who is engaged in or depicted as being engaged in explicit sexual activity in the presence of a child or a person depicted as being a child, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: a person exposing their sexual organs or anal region in the presence of a child or a person depicted as being a child;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation, written material or an audio recording in which or by means of which sexual activity between a person who is 18 years of age or more and a child is advocated, counselled or planned, other than one in which or by means of which sexual activity between a person who is 16 years of age or more but under 18 years of age and another person who is less than two years older than that person is advocated, counselled or planned;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation that shows a child who is being subjected to cruel, inhuman or degrading acts of physical violence;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: any excerpt of a visual representation referred to in paragraph (a), if it is reasonable to suspect that the communication of the excerpt perpetuates harm against a person who as a child appeared in the visual representation; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation, written material or an audio recording that, given the context in which it is communicated, is likely to bring to light a connection between a person and a visual representation, written material or audio recording referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (d) in which the person appeared as a child, if it is reasonable to suspect that the communication of the representation, material or recording that is likely to bring to light that connection perpetuates harm against the person.â(contenu reprÃ©sentant de la victimisation sexuelle dâenfants ou perpÃ©tuant la victimisation de survivants)

Definition.Text: content used to bully a childâmeans content, or an aggregate of content, that, given the context in which it is communicated, could cause serious harm to a childâs physical or mental health, if it is reasonable to suspect that the content or the aggregate of content is communicated for the purpose of threatening, intimidating or humiliating the child.â(contenu visant Ã intimider un enfant)
harmful contentâmeans

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: intimate content communicated without consent;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: content that induces a child to harm themselves;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: content used to bully a child;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: content that foments hatred;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: content that incites violence; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: content that incites violent extremism or terrorism.â(contenu prÃ©judiciable)

Definition.Text: Indigenous peoples of Canadaâhas the meaning assigned by the definition aboriginal peoples of Canada in subsection 35(2) of the Constitution Act, 1982.â(peuples autochtones du Canada)
intimate content communicated without consentâmeans

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a visual recording, such as a photographic, film or video recording, in which a person is nude or is exposing their sexual organs or anal region or is engaged in explicit sexual activity, if it is reasonable to suspect that

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the person had a reasonable expectation of privacy at the time of the recording, and

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the person does not consent to the recording being communicated; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a visual recording, such as a photographic, film or video recording, that falsely presents in a reasonably convincing manner a person as being nude or exposing their sexual organs or anal region or engaged in explicit sexual activity, including a deepfake that presents a person in that manner, if it is reasonable to suspect that the person does not consent to the recording being communicated.â(contenu intime communiquÃ© de faÃ§on non consensuelle)

Definition.Text: Ministerâmeans the member of the Kingâs Privy Council for Canada designated under section 8 or, if no member is designated, the Minister of Canadian Heritage.ââ(ministre)
Officeâmeans the Digital Safety Office of Canada established by section 39.â(Bureau)
Ombudspersonâmeans the Digital Safety Ombudsperson of Canada appointed under section 29.â(ombudsman)
operatorâmeans a person that, through any means, operates a regulated service.â(exploitant)
personâincludes a corporation and a trust, a partnership, a fund, a joint venture and any other unincorporated association or organization.â(personne)
personal informationâhas the same meaning as in section 3 of the Privacy Act.â(renseignements personnels)
regulated serviceâmeans a service referred to in subsection 3(1).â(service rÃ©glementÃ©)

social media serviceâmeans a website or application that is accessible in Canada, the primary purpose of which is to facilitate interprovincial or international online communication among users of the website or application by enabling them to access and share content.â(service de mÃ©dia social)
visual representationâincludes a visual recording such as a photographic, film or video recording.â(reprÃ©sentation)

Subsection.MarginalNote: For greater certainty â social media service
Subsection.Text: For greater certainty, a social media service includes

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: an adult content service, namely a social media service that is focused on enabling its users to access and share pornographic content; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a live streaming service, namely a social media service that is focused on enabling its users to access and share content by live stream.

Subsection.MarginalNote: For greater certainty â content that foments hatred
Subsection.Text: For greater certainty and for the purposes of the definition content that foments hatred, content does not express detestation or vilification solely because it expresses disdain or dislike or it discredits, humiliates, hurts or offends.
The definition content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor does not include

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation referred to in paragraph (a) of that definition whose creation and communication have a legitimate purpose related to the administration of justice or to science, medicine, education or art and do not pose an undue risk of harm to children;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation referred to in paragraph (f) of that definition whose creation or communication has a legitimate purpose referred to in paragraph (a) and does not pose an undue risk of harm to children;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: an excerpt of a visual representation referred to in paragraph (g) of that definition that is communicated for a legitimate purpose referred to in paragraph (a); and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: a visual representation, written material or an audio recording referred to in paragraph (h) of that definition that is communicated for a legitimate purpose referred to in paragraph (a).

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of this Act, a regulated service is a social media service that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: has a number of users that is equal to or greater than the significant number of users provided for by regulations made under subsection (2); or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: has a number of users that is less than the number of users provided for by regulations made under subsection (2) and is designated by regulations made under subsection (3).

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of subsection (1), the Governor in Council may make regulations

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: establishing types of social media services;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the number of users referred to in that subsection, for each type of social media service; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the manner of determining the number of users of a social media service.

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the Governor in Council may make regulations designating a particular social media service if the Governor in Council is satisfied that there is a significant risk that harmful content is accessible on the service.

At the Ministerâs request and within the time and in the manner specified by the Minister, the Commission must provide to the Minister any information that is relevant for the purposes of subsection (3).

Section.Text: At the Commissionâs request and within the time and in the manner specified by it, the person that, through any means, operates a social media service must provide to the Commission any information provided for by regulations, for the purpose of enabling the Commission to determine whether the social media service has a number of users that is equal to or greater than the number of users provided for by regulations made under subsection 3(2).

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of this Act, a service is not a social media service if it does not enable a user to communicate content to the public.

For the purposes of subsection (1), a service does not enable a user to communicate content to the public if it does not enable the user to communicate content to a potentially unlimited number of users not determined by the user.
The duties imposed under this Act on the operator of a regulated service do not apply in respect of any private messaging feature of the regulated service.
For the purposes of subsection (1), private messaging feature means a feature that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: enables a user to communicate content to a limited number of users determined by the user; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: does not enable a user to communicate content to a potentially unlimited number of users not determined by the user.

Section.MarginalNote: Proactive search of content not required

Subsection.Text: Nothing in this Act requires an operator to proactively search content on a regulated service that it operates in order to identify harmful content.

Despite subsection (1), regulations made under paragraph 140(1)(d) may require the operator of a regulated service to use technological means to prevent content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor from being uploaded to the service.

Section.Text: The Governor in Council may, by order, designate a member of the Kingâs Privy Council for Canada as the Minister for the purposes of this Act.
The purposes of this Act are to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: promote the online safety of persons in Canada;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: protect childrenâs physical and mental health;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: considering that exposure to harmful content online impacts the safety and well-being of persons in Canada, mitigate the risk that persons in Canada will be exposed to harmful content online while respecting their freedom of expression;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: enable persons in Canada to participate fully in public discourse and exercise their freedom of expression online without being hindered by harmful content;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: reduce harms caused to persons in Canada as a result of harmful content online;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: make content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor and intimate content communicated without consent inaccessible online;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: ensure that operators are transparent and accountable with respect to their duties under this Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: contribute to the development of standards with respect to online safety.

Section.Text: The Digital Safety Commission of Canada is established.
The Commissionâs mandate is to promote online safety in Canada and contribute to the reduction of harms caused to persons in Canada as a result of harmful content online by, among other things,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: ensuring the administration and enforcement of this Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: ensuring that operators are transparent and accountable with respect to their duties under this Act;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: investigating complaints relating to content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor and intimate content communicated without consent;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: contributing to the development of standards with respect to online safety through research and educational activities;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: facilitating the participation of Indigenous peoples of Canada and interested persons in the Commissionâs activities; and

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: collaborating with interested persons, including operators, the Commissionâs international counterparts and other persons having professional, technical or specialized knowledge.

Section.Text: The Commission consists of three to five full-time members to be appointed by the Governor in Council to hold office during good behaviour. The Governor in Council may remove a member for cause.

Members are to hold office for renewable terms of not more than five years that will ensure, to the extent possible, the end in any one calendar year of the terms of office of not more than half of the members.

A member is to be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council and is entitled to be paid, in accordance with Treasury Board directives, reasonable travel and living expenses incurred while absent from their ordinary place of work in the course of performing their duties and functions.

A member is deemed to be an employee for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and to be employed in the federal public administration for the purposes of regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.

A person is not eligible to be appointed or to continue as a member if the person is not a Canadian citizen or a permanent resident as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.

Section.MarginalNote: Incompatibility with other duties and functions
Section.Text: A member must devote the whole of their time to the performance of their duties and functions.

The Governor in Council must, after approval by resolution of the Senate and House of Commons, designate a member as Chairperson of the Commission. The Governor in Council may designate another member as Vice-chairperson of the Commission.
The Chairperson allocates and supervises the work of members and directs the work of the Commission.

Subsection.Text: The Chairperson may make rules respecting the Commissionâs procedures and practices.
The Commission must make the rules publicly available.

Section.Text: The Chairperson may authorize one or more members, either jointly or severally, to exercise any of the Commissionâs powers or perform any of its duties and functions.

The Chairperson may establish committees of members for the purposes of conducting the Commissionâs work and the Chairperson may authorize a committee of members to exercise any of the Commissionâs powers or perform any of its duties and functions.

The Vice-chairperson is to exercise any of the powers and perform any of the duties and functions that the Chairperson may assign to them and is to act as Chairperson if that office is vacant or if the Chairperson is absent or incapacitated.

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Chairperson and the Vice-chairperson or a vacancy in both those offices, the Minister may authorize another member to act as Chairperson, but no member so authorized has authority to act for a term of more than 90 days without the approval of the Governor in Council.

Subsection.Text: After consultation with the Chief Executive Officer of the Office, the Commission may delegate to employees of the Office, subject to any terms and conditions that the Commission may specify, the power to exercise any of the Commissionâs powers or perform any of its duties and functions.

The Commission is not authorized to delegate to employees of the Office the power to submit any report to the Minister or the Governor in Council, the power to make regulations that the Commission is authorized to make, the power to make orders under subsection 94(1) or the power to delegate under subsection (1).

Section.Text: The Commission may issue guidelines, codes of conduct and other such documents for the purposes of this Act.
When making regulations and issuing guidelines, codes of conduct and other documents, the Commission must take into account

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: freedom of expression;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: equality rights;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: privacy rights;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the needs and perspectives of the Indigenous peoples of Canada; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the Commission considers relevant.

Section.Text: The Commission must, on request of the Governor in Council, hold hearings or make reports on any matter that is within the Commissionâs mandate.
The Governor in Council is to appoint a Digital Safety Ombudsperson of Canada.
The Ombudsperson is to hold office during pleasure on a full-time basis for a renewable term of not more than five years.
The Ombudspersonâs mandate is to provide support to users of regulated services and advocate for the public interest with respect to systemic issues related to online safety.

The Ombudsperson is to be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council and is entitled to be paid, in accordance with Treasury Board directives, reasonable travel and living expenses incurred while absent from their ordinary place of work in the course of performing their duties and functions.

The Ombudsperson is deemed to be an employee for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and to be employed in the federal public administration for the purposes of regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.

A person is not eligible to be appointed or to continue as Ombudsperson if the person is not a Canadian citizen or a permanent resident as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.

Section.MarginalNote: Incompatibility with other duties and functions
Section.Text: The Ombudsperson must devote the whole of their time to the performance of their duties and functions.

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Ombudsperson or a vacancy in that office, the Minister may authorize any person to act as Ombudsperson, but no one so authorized has authority to act for a term of more than 90 days without the approval of the Governor in Council.
In fulfilling their mandate under section 31, the Ombudsperson may

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: gather information with respect to issues related to online safety, including with respect to harmful content, such as by obtaining the perspective of users of regulated services and victims of harmful content;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: highlight issues related to online safety, including by making publicly available any information gathered under paragraph (a), other than personal information; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: direct users to resources, including those provided for under this Act, that may address their concerns regarding harmful content.

Section.Text: After consultation with the Chief Executive Officer of the Office, the Ombudsperson may delegate to employees of the Office, subject to any terms and conditions that the Ombudsperson may specify, the power to exercise any of the Ombudspersonâs powers or perform any of their duties and functions, except the power to submit any report to the Minister or the power to delegate under this section.
The Digital Safety Office of Canada is established.
The Officeâs mandate is to support the Commission and the Ombudsperson in the fulfillment of their mandates, the exercise of their powers and the performance of their duties and functions.
The head office of the Office is to be at any place in Canada that may be designated by the Governor in Council.
On the recommendation of the Minister, the Governor in Council is to appoint a Chief Executive Officer of the Office.
The Chief Executive Officer has the rank and status of a deputy head of a department.
The Chief Executive Officer is to hold office during pleasure on a full-time basis for a renewable term of not more than five years.

Subsection.Text: The Chief Executive Officer is responsible for the management of the Officeâs day-to-day business and affairs, including the supervision of its employees and their work.
The Chief Executive Officer must not give employees of the Office any directions with respect to any particular decision, order or recommendation that is made by the Commission or the Ombudsperson.

Section.Text: The Chief Executive Officer may enter into contracts, memoranda of understanding or other arrangements, including contracts to engage the services of legal counsel or other persons having professional, technical or specialized knowledge to advise or assist the Commission or Ombudsperson.

The Chief Executive Officer is to be paid the remuneration that is fixed by the Governor in Council and is entitled to be paid, in accordance with Treasury Board directives, reasonable travel and living expenses incurred while absent from their ordinary place of work in the course of performing their duties and functions.

The Chief Executive Officer is deemed to be an employee for the purposes of the Government Employees Compensation Act and to be employed in the federal public administration for the purposes of regulations made under section 9 of the Aeronautics Act.

A person is not eligible to be appointed or to continue as Chief Executive Officer if the person is not a Canadian citizen or a permanent resident as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.

Section.MarginalNote: Incompatibility with other duties and functions
Section.Text: The Chief Executive Officer must devote the whole of their time to the performance of their duties and functions.

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the Chief Executive Officer or a vacancy in that office, the Minister may authorize any person to act as Chief Executive Officer, but no one so authorized has authority to act for a term of more than 90 days without the approval of the Governor in Council.

Subsection.Text: The Chief Executive Officer may employ any employees that are necessary to conduct the work of the Office.
Employees are to be appointed in accordance with the Public Service Employment Act.

Section.Text: The Chief Executive Officer may delegate to employees of the Office, subject to any terms and conditions that the Chief Executive Officer may specify, the power to exercise any of the Chief Executive Officerâs powers or perform any of their duties and functions, except the power to delegate under this section.

Heading.Label: PART 4
Heading.TitleText: Duties of Operators of Regulated Services

Section.Text: An operator has a duty to act responsibly in respect of a regulated service that it operates by complying with sections 55 to 63 and with orders made by the Commission under subsection 74(1).

Subsection.Text: The operator of a regulated service must implement measures that are adequate to mitigate the risk that users of the service will be exposed to harmful content on the service.

In order to determine whether the measures implemented by the operator are adequate to mitigate the risk that users of the regulated service will be exposed to harmful content on the service, the Commission must take into account the following factors:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the effectiveness of the measures in mitigating the risk;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the size of the service, including the number of users;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the technical and financial capacity of the operator;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: whether the measures are designed or implemented in a manner that is discriminatory on the basis of a prohibited ground of discrimination within the meaning of the Canadian Human Rights Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: any factor provided for by regulations.

Subsection.MarginalNote: No unreasonable or disproportionate limit on expression
Subsection.Text: Subsection (1) does not require the operator to implement measures that unreasonably or disproportionately limit usersâ expression on the regulated service.

Section.Text: The operator of a regulated service must implement any measures that are provided for by regulations to mitigate the risk that users of the service will be exposed to harmful content on the service.
The operator of a regulated service must make user guidelines publicly available on the service. The user guidelines must be accessible and easy to use and must include

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a standard of conduct that applies to users with respect to harmful content; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a description of the measures that the operator implements with respect to harmful content on the service.

Section.Text: The operator of a regulated service must make available to users who have an account or are otherwise registered with the service tools that enable those users to block other users who have an account or are otherwise registered with the service from finding or communicating with them on the service.

Section.MarginalNote: Tools and processes to flag harmful content

Subsection.Text: The operator of a regulated service must implement tools and processes to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: enable a user to easily flag to the operator content that is accessible on the service as being a particular type of harmful content;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: notify a user who flagged content as being a particular type of harmful content of the operatorâs receipt of the flag as well as of any measures taken by the operator with respect to the content or of the fact that no measures were taken; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: notify a user who communicated content that was flagged as being a particular type of harmful content of the fact that the content was flagged as well as of any measures taken by the operator with respect to the content or of the fact that no measures were taken.

Subsection.Text: In notifying a user of any measures taken with respect to content in accordance with paragraphs (1)(b) and (c), the operator must not notify a user of any report that the operator has made to a law enforcement agency in relation to the content.

Section.MarginalNote: Multiple instances of automated communication by computer program
Section.Text: The operator of a regulated service must label, as being content that is described in this section, harmful content â other than content that is referred to in subsections 67(1) and 68(1) â that is accessible on the service if the operator has reasonable grounds to believe that the content

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: is the subject of multiple instances of automated communication on the service by a computer program, other than a computer program that is implemented by the operator to facilitate the proper functioning of the service; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: is more prominent on the service than it would have been had it not been the subject of those multiple instances of automated communication by a computer program.

Subsection.Text: The operator of a regulated service must make a resource person available to users of the service to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: hear usersâ concerns with respect to harmful content on the service or with respect to the measures that the operator implements to comply with this Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: direct users to internal and external resources to address their concerns, such as an internal complaints mechanism, the Commission or a law enforcement agency; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: provide guidance to users with respect to those internal resources.

Subsection.Text: The operator must ensure that the resource person is easily identifiable and that the resource personâs contact information is easily accessible to users of the service.

The operator of a regulated service must submit a digital safety plan to the Commission in respect of each regulated service that it operates. The digital safety plan must include the following information in respect of the period provided for in the regulations:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the manner in which the operator complies with sections 55 and 56, including

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the operatorâs assessment of the risk that users of the service will be exposed to harmful content on the service,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a description of the measures that the operator implements to mitigate the risk,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: the operatorâs assessment of the effectiveness of the measures â both individually and collectively â in mitigating the risk,

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: a description of the indicators that the operator uses to assess the effectiveness of the measures, and

Subparagraph.Label: (v)
Subparagraph.Text: information respecting the factors referred to in subsection 55(2);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the manner in which the operator complies with sections 57 to 61, including a description of the measures that the operator implements under those sections;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the manner in which the operator complies with section 65, including a description of the design features that the operator integrates into the service under that section;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting any measures that the operator implements to protect children, other than those that it implements under section 65;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the resources, including human resources, that the operator allocates in order to comply with sections 55 to 61 and 65, including information respecting the resources that the operator allocates to automated decision-making;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the volume and type of harmful content that was accessible on the service, including the volume and type of harmful content that was moderated, and the volume and type of harmful content that would have been accessible on the service had it not been moderated, as well as the manner in which and the time within which harmful content was moderated;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the number of times that content that was accessible on the service was flagged to the operator by users of the service as being harmful content, including the number of flags relating to each type of harmful content,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the manner in which the operator triaged and assessed the flags,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: measures taken by the operator with respect to content that was flagged as being harmful content, and

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: the time within which the operator took measures with respect to content that was flagged as being harmful content;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the content, other than harmful content, that was moderated by the operator and that the operator had reasonable grounds to believe posed a risk of significant psychological or physical harm, including

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: a description of the content,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the volume of the content that was accessible on the service or that would have been accessible had it not been moderated, and

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: the manner in which and the time within which the content was moderated;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the concerns heard by the resource person referred to in subsection 61(1) and the internal and external resources to which the resource person directed users for the purposes of that subsection;

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the topics, and a summary of the findings, conclusions or recommendations, of any research conducted by or on behalf of the operator with respect to

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: harmful content on the service,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: content on the service that poses a risk of significant psychological or physical harm, other than harmful content, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: design features of the service that pose a risk of significant psychological or physical harm;

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the measures implemented by the operator for the purposes of complying with the operatorâs duties with respect to the service under An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service;

Paragraph.Label: (l)
Paragraph.Text: an inventory of all electronic data, other than content that was communicated by users on the service, that was used to prepare the information referred to in paragraphs (a) to (g), (i) and (m); and

Paragraph.Label: (m)
Paragraph.Text: any other information provided for by regulations.

Subsection.Text: The operator must ensure that the digital safety plan does not contain any personal information.

For greater certainty, the digital safety plan must not contain any information that must not be disclosed under section 5 of An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service.
The operator must make the digital safety plan publicly available on the service to which the plan relates in an accessible and easy-to-read format.
The operator is not required to include any of the following information in the digital safety plan that the operator makes publicly available:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the inventory of electronic data referred to in paragraph (1)(l);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: information that is a trade secret; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: financial, commercial, scientific or technical information that is confidential and that is treated consistently in a confidential manner by the person to whose business or affairs it relates.

Section.MarginalNote: Duty to preserve certain harmful content

Subsection.Text: If the operator of a regulated service makes inaccessible to all persons in Canada content that incites violence or content that incites violent extremism or terrorism, the operator must preserve that content, and all other computer data related to it that is in the operatorâs possession or control, for a period of one year beginning on the day on which the content is made inaccessible.

After the end of the one-year period, the operator must, as soon as feasible, destroy the content and any other computer data related to it that would not be retained in the ordinary course of business, as well as any document that was prepared for the purpose of preserving that content and data, unless the operator is required to preserve the content, data or document under a judicial order made under any other Act of Parliament or an Act of the legislature of a province or under a preservation demand made under section 487.012 of the Criminal Code.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Exception â content never made accessible
Subsection.Text: Subsections (1) and (2) do not apply to content that was never made accessible on the regulated service.
In this section, computer data has the same meaning as in subsection 1(1) of An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service.

Section.Text: An operator has a duty, in respect of a regulated service that it operates, to protect children by complying with section 65.
An operator must integrate into a regulated service that it operates any design features respecting the protection of children, such as age appropriate design, that are provided for by regulations.

The Commission may establish guidelines respecting the protection of children in relation to regulated services and respecting the manner in which the operator of a regulated service may comply with regulations made under paragraph 140(1)(o). The guidelines are established for information purposes only.

Heading.TitleText: Duty to Make Certain Content Inaccessible

Subsection.Text: If the operator of a regulated service identifies on the service, other than as a result of a flag by a user, content that the operator has reasonable grounds to suspect is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the operator

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: must make that content inaccessible to all persons in Canada within the period that applies under subsection (2) and continue to make it inaccessible until the operator makes a decision under subsection 69(2); and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: must, within the period that applies under subsection (2), give notice to the user who communicated the content on the service that the content has been made inaccessible.

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of subsection (1), the period is the period of 24 hours â or, if a period of a different length is provided for by regulations, the period of that length â after the operator identifies the content.

If a user of a regulated service flags to the operator content on the service as being content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the operator must, within the period that applies under subsection (5), conduct an initial assessment of the flag and dismiss the flag if the operator is of the opinion that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the flag is trivial, frivolous, vexatious or made in bad faith; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the content that is the subject of the flag is or has already been the subject of another flag and further assessment of the flag is unnecessary having regard to all of the circumstances.

Subsection.Text: If the operator dismisses the flag, it must, within the period that applies under subsection (5), notify the user who flagged the content of the dismissal.

If the operator does not dismiss the flag, the operator must make the content inaccessible to all persons in Canada within the period that applies under subsection (5) and continue to make it inaccessible until the operator makes a decision under subsection 69(2).

If the operator makes content inaccessible under subsection (3), the operator must, within the period that applies under subsection (5), give notice to the user who communicated the content on the service and the user who flagged the content that the content has been made inaccessible.

For the purposes of subsections (1) to (4), the period is the period of 24 hours â or, if a period of a different length is provided for by regulations, the period of that length â after the content is flagged by a user.

If the operator of a regulated service makes content inaccessible under subsection 67(1) or 68(3), the operator must, within the period provided for by regulations, give the user who communicated the content on the service and the user who flagged the content, if applicable, an opportunity to make representations as to whether the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.

As soon as feasible after the end of the period provided for by regulations for making representations or, if a different period is provided for by regulations, within that period, the operator must decide whether there are reasonable grounds to believe that the content that was made inaccessible to all persons in Canada is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.
As soon as feasible after making a decision, the operator must give notice of the decision to the user who communicated the content on the service and the user who flagged the content, if applicable.

If the operator decides that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the operator must continue to make the content inaccessible to all persons in Canada.

At the request of the user who communicated the content on the service or the user who flagged the content, the operator of a regulated service must reconsider a decision that it made under subsection 69(2).

The operator must, within the period provided for by regulations, give the user who communicated the content on the service and the user who flagged the content, if applicable, an opportunity to make representations as to whether the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.

As soon as feasible after the end of the period provided for by regulations for making representations or, if a different period is provided for by regulations, within that period, the operator must reconsider a decision made under subsection 69(2).

As soon as feasible after making a decision under subsection (3), the operator must give notice of the decision to the user who communicated the content on the service and the user who flagged the content, if applicable.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Duty to continue to make inaccessible ceases
Subsection.Text: If the operator decides, on reconsideration under subsection (3), that there are no reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the operatorâs duty to continue to make the content inaccessible under subsection 69(4), if applicable, ceases.

If the operator decides, on reconsideration under subsection (3), that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the operator must

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: make the content inaccessible to all persons in Canada without delay, if subsection 69(4) does not already apply to the operator in respect of the content; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: continue to make the content inaccessible to all persons in Canada if subsection 69(4) applies.

Section.Text: For greater certainty, nothing in sections 67 to 70 affects any duty that the operator of a regulated service may have under An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service.

The operator of a regulated service must keep all records, including information and data, that are necessary to determine whether the operator is complying with the operatorâs duties under this Act.

Heading.Label: PART 5
Heading.TitleText: Access to Inventories and Electronic Data

Subsection.Text: The Commission may, on request and in accordance with the criteria set out in the regulations, accredit a person, other than an individual, for the purpose of giving that person access to the inventories of electronic data that are included in digital safety plans that have been submitted to the Commission under subsection 62(1) if the Commission determines that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the personâs primary purpose is to conduct research or engage in education, advocacy or awareness activities; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the person conducts research that is, or engages in education, advocacy or awareness activities that are, related to the purposes of this Act.

Subsection.Text: The Commission may give a person that is accredited under subsection (1) access to the inventories of electronic data referred to in that subsection.

After giving an accredited person a reasonable opportunity to make representations, the Commission may suspend or revoke the accreditation if the Commission determines that the accredited person failed to comply with any condition under this Act that relates to the accreditation, to access to the inventories of electronic data or to access to the electronic data.

Section.MarginalNote: Order â access to electronic data

Subsection.Text: At the request of an accredited person and in accordance with the criteria set out in the regulations, the Commission may make an order requiring the operator of a regulated service to give individuals who are referred to in the request access to any electronic data that is referred to in an inventory of electronic data that is included in a digital safety plan that has been submitted to the Commission under subsection 62(1) in respect of the regulated service and that is specified in the request.
An accredited person may make a request under subsection (1) only in respect of a research project that is related to the purposes of this Act.
A request must include

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a description of the research project in respect of which access to electronic data is requested and a description of how the research project is related to the purposes of this Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: information respecting the individuals who are undertaking the research project and for whom access to electronic data is requested;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the name of the operator from which access to electronic data is requested and the name of the regulated service to which the electronic data relates;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: a description of the electronic data to which access is requested, which must be referred to in an inventory of electronic data that is included in a digital safety plan that has been submitted to the Commission under subsection 62(1) in respect of the regulated service;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: an indication of the time within which access to the electronic data is requested;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: an indication of the length of time for which access to the electronic data is requested;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: a description of how any conditions set out in the regulations with respect to confidentiality, intellectual property, data security and the protection of personal information will be met; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: any other information provided for by regulations.

Subsection.Text: An order made under subsection (1) must specify

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the name of the regulated service to which it relates;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the individuals referred to in paragraph (3)(b) to whom access to the electronic data must be given;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the electronic data referred to in paragraph (3)(d) to which access must be given;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the time within which access to the electronic data must be given;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the length of time for which access to the electronic data must be given;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: any conditions with respect to confidentiality, intellectual property, data security and the protection of personal information;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: any other conditions that the Commission considers appropriate; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: any other information provided for by regulations.

Subsection.Text: For greater certainty, in complying with an order, the operator must not disclose any information that must not be disclosed under section 5 of An Act respecting the mandatory reporting of Internet child pornography by persons who provide an Internet service.
The Statutory Instruments Act does not apply to an order made under subsection (1).

Section.Text: At the request of the operator of a regulated service that is the subject of an order made under subsection 74(1), the Commission may amend or revoke the order if the Commission determines, in accordance with the criteria set out in the regulations, that the operator is unable to comply with the order or that complying with it would cause the operator undue hardship.

Subsection.Text: The accredited person at whose request an order was made under subsection 74(1) may make a complaint to the Commission that the operator that is the subject of the order failed to comply with the order.
The Commission must give the operator a reasonable opportunity to make representations with respect to the complaint.

Section.Text: The Commission may publish a list of persons that are accredited under subsection 73(1) and a description of the research projects in respect of which the Commission has made an order under subsection 74(1).

Subsection.Text: A person in Canada may make submissions to the Commission respecting harmful content that is accessible on a regulated service or the measures taken by the operator of a regulated service to comply with the operatorâs duties under this Act.
The Commission may inform an operator of any submissions that it receives under subsection (1), in a manner that protects the identity of the person who made the submissions.

The Commission may make public information respecting any submissions that it receives under subsection (1), in a manner that protects the identity of the person who made the submissions and the regulated service in respect of which the submissions were made.

If an employee of the operator of a regulated service makes submissions under subsection 78(1) with respect to the service, the employee may make a request to the Commission that the following information be kept confidential:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the employeeâs identity; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any information in the submissions that the employee identifies as being likely to reveal their identity.

Subsection.Text: It is prohibited for an individual referred to in subsection (3) to disclose information that an employee has requested be kept confidential under subsection (1), or allow it to be disclosed, other than to the following individuals in the course of exercising the Commissionâs powers or performing its duties and functions under this Act:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a member of the Commission or any individual acting on behalf of the Commission or under its direction;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: an employee of the Office; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the Chief Executive Officer of the Office.

Subsection.Text: Subsection (2) applies to any individual referred to in any of the following paragraphs who comes into possession of the information while holding the office or employment described in that paragraph, whether or not the individual has ceased to hold that office or be so employed:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a member of the Commission or any individual acting on behalf of the Commission or under its direction;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: an employee of the Office; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the Chief Executive Officer of the Office.

Subsection.Text: Subsection (2) does not apply with respect to the following information:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: information in respect of which the employee withdraws the request;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: information that is publicly available; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information that the Commission obtains from a source other than the submissions made by the employee.

Subsection.Text: The Commission may disclose information referred to in subsection (1) to the Minister if required to do so under subsection 138(1).
Every individual who contravenes subsection 79(2) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: for a first offence, to a fine of not more than $5,000; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: for a second or subsequent offence, to a fine of not more than $10,000.

Subsection.Text: An individual is not to be found guilty of an offence under subsection (1) if they establish that they exercised due diligence to prevent the commission of the offence.

A person in Canada may make a complaint to the Commission that content on a regulated service is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.
If a person makes a complaint to the Commission, the Commission must conduct an initial assessment of the complaint and dismiss it if the Commission is of the opinion that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the complaint is trivial, frivolous, vexatious or made in bad faith; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the content that is the subject of the complaint is or has already been the subject of another complaint and further assessment of the complaint is unnecessary having regard to all of the circumstances.

Subsection.Text: If the Commission dismisses the complaint, it must give the complainant notice of the dismissal.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Notice and interim order to make content inaccessible
Subsection.Text: If the Commission does not dismiss the complaint, it must

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: give notice of the complaint to the operator of the regulated service to which the complaint relates and the user who communicated the content on the service; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: make an order requiring the operator to, without delay, make the content inaccessible to all persons in Canada and to continue to make it inaccessible until the Commission gives notice to the operator of its decision under subsection 82(4) or (5), as the case may be.

Subsection.Text: The operator must give notice to the Commission and the user who communicated the content on the service that the content has been made inaccessible in accordance with an order made under paragraph (4)(b).

The operator must ask the user who communicated the content on the service whether they consent to the operator providing the userâs contact information to the Commission. If the user consents, the operator must provide the contact information to the Commission.

If the Commission makes an order under paragraph 81(4)(b), the Commission must give the complainant and the user who communicated the content on the service an opportunity to make representations as to whether the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.

The Commission must decide whether there are reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent.
The Commission must give notice of the decision to the complainant and the user who communicated the content on the service.

If the Commission decides that there are no reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the Commission must give the operator notice of the decision and of the fact that the order made under paragraph 81(4)(b) requiring the operator to continue to make the content inaccessible ceases to have effect.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Notice and order to make content permanently inaccessible
Subsection.Text: If the Commission decides that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the content is content that sexually victimizes a child or revictimizes a survivor or intimate content communicated without consent, the Commission must give the operator notice of the decision and make an order requiring the operator to make the content permanently inaccessible to all persons in Canada.

The operator must give notice to the Commission and the user who communicated the content on the service that the content has been made inaccessible in accordance with an order made under subsection (5).

Section.Text: Any duty imposed on the Commission under paragraph 81(4)(a) or subsection 82(1) or (3) in respect of a user who communicated content on a regulated service applies only if the Commission has the userâs contact information.

At the request of the Commission and within the time and in the manner that it specifies, the operator of a regulated service in respect of which a complaint has been made under subsection 81(1) must provide to the Commission any information that the Commission considers necessary to investigate the complaint, other than personal information.
The Statutory Instruments Act does not apply to an order made under paragraph 81(4)(b) or subsection 82(5).
In ensuring an operatorâs compliance with this Act or investigating a complaint made under subsection 81(1), the Commission may, in accordance with any rules made under subsection 20(1),

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: summon and enforce the appearance of persons before the Commission and compel them to give oral or written evidence on oath and to produce any documents or other things that the Commission considers necessary, in the same manner and to the same extent as a superior court of record;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: administer oaths;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: receive and accept any evidence or other information, whether on oath, by affidavit or otherwise, that the Commission sees fit, whether or not it would be admissible in a court of law; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: decide any procedural or evidentiary question.

Section.Text: The Commission is not bound by any legal or technical rules of evidence. It must deal with all matters that come before it as informally and expeditiously as the circumstances and considerations of fairness and natural justice permit.

Subsection.Text: The Commission may hold a hearing, in accordance with any rules made under subsection 20(1), in connection with

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a complaint made under subsection 81(1); or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any other matter relating to an operatorâs compliance with this Act.

Subsection.Text: A hearing must be held in public, but the Commission may decide that it is to be held in private, in whole or in part, if the Commission considers that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: it would be in the public interest;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: it would be in the interest of victims of harmful content;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: it would be in the national interest, including if there is a risk of injury to Canadaâs international relations, national defence or national security;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: a personâs privacy interest outweighs the principle that hearings be open to the public; or

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the following information may be disclosed:

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: information that is a trade secret,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: financial, commercial, scientific or technical information that is confidential and that is treated consistently in a confidential manner by the person to whose business or affairs it relates, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: information whose disclosure could reasonably be expected to

Clause.Label: (A)
Clause.Text: result in material financial loss or gain to any person,

Clause.Label: (B)
Clause.Text: prejudice the competitive position of any person, or

Clause.Label: (C)
Clause.Text: affect contractual or other negotiations of any person.

Section.Text: A hearing is to be held at any time and at any place in Canada and in the manner that the Commission considers appropriate for the exercise of its powers or the performance of its duties and functions.

Subsection.Text: The Commission may designate as inspectors persons or classes of persons that the Commission considers qualified for the purposes of verifying compliance or preventing non-compliance with this Act.

The Commission must provide every inspector with a certificate of designation. An inspector must, if requested to do so, produce their certificate to the person appearing to be in charge of any place that they enter.

Subject to subsection 92(1), an inspector may, for a purpose related to verifying compliance or preventing non-compliance with this Act, enter any place in which they have reasonable grounds to believe that there is any document, information or other thing relevant to that purpose.
An inspector is considered to have entered a place if they access it remotely by a means of telecommunication.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Limitation â access by means of telecommunication
Subsection.Text: An inspector who enters remotely, by a means of telecommunication, a place that is not accessible to the public must do so with the knowledge of the owner or person in charge of the place and must be remotely in the place for no longer than the period necessary for the purpose referred to in subsection (1).
An inspector may, for the purpose referred to in subsection (1),

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: examine any document or information that is found in the place, copy it in whole or in part and take it for examination or copying;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: examine any other thing that is found in the place and take it for examination;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: use or cause to be used any computer system at the place to examine any document or information that is found in the place;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: reproduce any document or information or cause it to be reproduced and take it for examination or copying; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: use or cause to be used any copying equipment or means of telecommunication at the place to make copies of or transmit any document or information.

Subsection.Text: The owner or person in charge of the place and every person in the place must give all assistance that is reasonably required to enable the inspector to exercise their powers or perform their duties and functions under this section and must provide any document, information or other thing that is reasonably required for that purpose.
An inspector may be accompanied by any other individual that the inspector believes is necessary to help them exercise their powers or perform their duties and functions under this section.

If the place referred to in subsection 91(1) is a dwelling-house, an inspector is not authorized to enter it without the occupantâs consent except under the authority of a warrant issued under subsection (2).

A justice of the peace may, on ex parte application, issue a warrant authorizing an inspector to enter a dwelling-house, subject to any conditions specified in the warrant, if the justice of the peace is satisfied by information on oath that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the dwelling-house is a place referred to in subsection 91(1);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: entry to the dwelling-house is necessary for the purpose referred to in that subsection; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: entry to the dwelling-house was refused by the occupant or there are reasonable grounds to believe that it will be refused by, or that consent to enter cannot be obtained from, the occupant.

Subsection.Text: An application for a warrant under subsection (2) may be submitted, and the warrant may be issued, by a means of telecommunication and section 487.1 of the Criminal Code applies for those purposes with any necessary modifications.
An inspector is not entitled to use force in executing a warrant unless the use of force has been specifically authorized in the warrant and the inspector is accompanied by a peace officer.

Section.MarginalNote: Requirement to provide information or access
Section.Text: An inspector may, for a purpose related to verifying compliance or preventing non-compliance with this Act, require any person who is in possession of a document or information that the inspector considers necessary for that purpose to provide the document or information to the inspector or provide the inspector with access to the document or information, in the form and manner and within the time specified by the inspector.

Subsection.Text: If the Commission has reasonable grounds to believe that an operator is contravening or has contravened this Act, it may make an order requiring the operator to take, or refrain from taking, any measure to ensure compliance with this Act.
The Statutory Instruments Act does not apply to an order made under subsection (1).
An order of the Commission may be made an order of the Federal Court and is enforceable in the same manner as an order of that court.
An order may be made an order of the Federal Court by following the usual practice and procedure of that court or by filing a certified copy of the order with the registrar of that court.
Subject to the regulations, an operator commits a violation and is liable to an administrative monetary penalty if it

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes a provision of this Act or the regulations;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes an order of the Commission;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes a requirement imposed by an inspector under section 93;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes an undertaking that it entered into with the Commission or a person authorized to enter into undertakings;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes a requirement imposed by the Commission under section 117 or subsection 119(2);

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: obstructs or hinders the Commission, an inspector or a person authorized to issue a notice of violation, in the exercise of their powers or the performance of their duties and functions; or

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: makes a false or misleading statement orally or in writing to the Commission, an inspector or a person authorized to issue a notice of violation, in the exercise of their powers or the performance of their duties and functions.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Person that operates social media service
Subsection.Text: Subject to the regulations, a person that, through any means, operates a social media service â other than an operator â commits a violation and is liable to an administrative monetary penalty if the person

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes section 4;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: contravenes a requirement imposed by an inspector under section 93;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: obstructs or hinders the Commission, an inspector or a person authorized to issue a notice of violation, in the exercise of their powers or the performance of their duties and functions; or

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: makes a false or misleading statement orally or in writing to the Commission, an inspector or a person authorized to issue a notice of violation, in the exercise of their powers or the performance of their duties and functions.
